data.content_ref: inline:{"claim": "The collapse was driven by the militarization of marginal populations using new infantry tactics that overwhelmed chariot-based aristocracies.", "mechanism": "The diffusion of iron-working and light infantry tactics (javelin throwers, slashers) empowered decentralized groups ('Sea Peoples') and internal dissidents. The palace economies, optimized for expensive chariot warfare and reliant on a small warrior elite, could not mobilize quickly enough against fast-moving, low-cost infantry raids, causing a rapid cascade of political legitimacy failures.", "scope": {"covers": ["Mycenaean Greece", "Hittite Anatolia", "Ugarit", "Levantine city-states", "Sea Peoples"], "excludes": ["Egypt's core Nile valley"]}, "forbidden": [{"case": "Evidence demonstrates that Mycenaean or Hittite armies successfully adopted light infantry tactics prior to their final destruction.", "eval": "rubric:std-hist"}], "prose_notes": "A standard military-diffusion explanation, focusing on the shift from chariot to infantry dominance."}
